label: Eq. 1
verbatim: h(x) := sum_{k in T(x)} g_k(x) f_k(x)
paraphrase: SMoE layer output is the gate-weighted sum of expert outputs over the top-k routed expert set T(x).
preconditions: K experts f_k:R^d->R^d; sparse gate g(x) in R^K_{>=0} with g_k(x)>0 iff k in T(x); active gates normalized: sum_{k in T(x)} g_k(x)=1.
consequences: Defines the baseline output that compressed layers must approximate.

label: Eq. 2
verbatim: h_bar(x) := sum_{k != j} (g_k(x) / (1 - g_j(x))) f_k(x)
paraphrase: Pruned-layer output: drop expert j, renormalize the remaining gates by 1/(1 - g_j(x)).
preconditions: Pruning expert j from the top-k active set.
consequences: Pruning preserves independent gate control over surviving experts; gates are renormalized, not tied.

label: Eq. 3
verbatim: h_tilde(x) := (g_i(x) + g_j(x)) f_tilde(x) + sum_{k != i,j} g_k(x) f_k(x)
paraphrase: Merged-layer output: experts (i,j) replaced by single merged expert f_tilde, gates summed.
preconditions: Existing one-shot merging methods (HC-SMoE, M-SMoE) sum the gates of merged experts.
consequences: Summed-gate merging ties the two gates; router loses ability to independently modulate i vs j.

label: Eq. 4
verbatim: g_i f_i + g_j f_j = (g_i + g_j) [ r(x) f_i(x) + (1 - r(x)) f_j(x) ]   where r(x) := g_i(x) / (g_i(x) + g_j(x))
paraphrase: Original pair contribution decomposes into summed gate (g_i+g_j) times an input-dependent convex mixture with ratio r(x).
preconditions: Local set where g_i + g_j > 0; r(x) in [0,1].
consequences: Identifies the dynamic input-dependent target expert that any constant-alpha merge must approximate.

label: Eq. 5
verbatim: E_merge = E_x[ (g_i + g_j)^2 * (r - alpha*)^2 * ||Delta_ij||^2 ]   with alpha* = E[r(x)],  Delta_ij := f_i - f_j
paraphrase: Minimum mean-squared merge error decomposes into router-scale (g_i+g_j)^2, policy variability (r-alpha*)^2, and expert gap ||Delta_ij||^2.
preconditions: f_tilde(x) = alpha f_i(x) + (1-alpha) f_j(x) with constant alpha; experts modelled as linear in x; alpha minimized at expected mixing ratio.
consequences: Establishes the irreducible-error bound: any constant-alpha merge incurs positive error if Var[r]>0 and ||Delta_ij||>0.

label: Eq. 6
verbatim: E_x[(g_i+g_j)^2 (r-alpha*)^2 ||Delta_ij||^2] ~ E_x[(g_i+g_j)^2] * Var[r(x)] * G_ij,   G_ij := E_x[||Delta_ij(x)||^2]
paraphrase: Under a weak-correlation assumption between router scale, policy variability, and expert gap, the merge error factorizes into the product of these three expectations.
preconditions: Router scale (g_i+g_j)^2, policy variability (r-alpha*)^2, and G_ij are weakly correlated across inputs.
consequences: Summed-gate merging is fundamentally flawed when (i) Var[r] > 0 and (ii) ||Delta_ij|| > 0.

label: Eq. 7
verbatim: E_prune = E_{x | j in T(x)} || (g_j(x) f_j(x) - g'_i(x) f_i(x)) - ((g_j(x) - g'_i(x))/(1 - g_j(x) + g'_i(x))) * sum_{k != i,j} g_k(x) f_k(x) ||^2
paraphrase: Pruning error decomposes into a substitution-error term (promoted expert i replacing pruned expert j) and a renormalization-error term scaling the surviving experts.
preconditions: Pruning expert j with j in T(x); promoted previously-inactive expert i acquires new gate g'_i(x) != 0.
consequences: Substitution error dominates; renormalization only rescales surviving expert magnitudes without changing direction.

label: Eq. 8
paraphrase: Upper bound on the pruning substitution error: gated triangle inequality with maximum at g'_i ~ g_j.
preconditions: Top-k routing implies g'_i <= g_j; max substitution error when g'_i ~ g_j.
consequences: This bound is the optimization target REAP minimizes: select pruned experts to keep g_j(x)||f_j(x)|| small.

label: Eq. 9 (REAP saliency)
verbatim: S_j = (1 / |X_j|) * sum_{x in X_j} g_j(x) * ||f_j(x)||_2,   where X_j = { x | j in T(x) }
paraphrase: REAP saliency: per-expert mean of router-gate times expert-activation L2-norm, averaged ONLY over tokens where the expert is active (conditional, not global).
preconditions: X_j is the set of calibration tokens for which expert j is in the top-k. Conditional averaging over X_j (not over the full calibration set X_cal) decouples functional impact from activation frequency.
consequences: CRITICAL FORMULA. Pruning experts with the lowest S_j minimizes the upper bound on substitution error (Eq. 8). Used by Stage 2 of the spec.

label: REAP norm choice
paraphrase: REAP uses the L2 (Euclidean) norm of the expert output activation vector, weighted by the (normalized, post-softmax) router gate value.
preconditions: Active gates assumed normalized to sum to 1 per token (§3 Setup).
consequences: Implementations must apply the SAME normalization the model uses (e.g. softmax over top-k) and the L2 norm, not L1 / squared-L2 / Frobenius.

label: REAP conditional averaging
verbatim: Crucially, calculating this average conditionally over X_j rather than globally decouples the expert's functional impact from its frequency of activation. A global average may be dominated by usage frequency and risks pruning specialist experts which are rarely activated but contribute significantly to the layer output when selected.
paraphrase: Average must be computed by dividing by |X_j| (count of tokens where expert j was active), not by the total calibration token count.
preconditions: Expert j has been activated at least once in X_cal (X_j non-empty).
consequences: Distinguishes REAP from EAN-style global accumulation; specialist (low-frequency, high-magnitude) experts are protected.

label: Eq. 10
verbatim: sum_{j in C} g_{i_j}(x) f_{i_j}(x) = ( sum_{j in C} g_{i_j}(x) ) * ( sum_{j in C} w_j(x) f_{i_j}(x) ),   w_j(x) = g_{i_j}(x) / sum_{l in C} g_{i_l}(x)
paraphrase: For a hierarchical cluster C of k experts, the original summed contribution decomposes into the cluster's total gate and a dynamic, input-dependent within-cluster mixture w_j(x).
preconditions: Cluster C = {f_{i_1},...,f_{i_k}}; weights w_j sum to 1.
consequences: Generalises Eq. 4 to clusters of size >2; sets up the hierarchical irreducible-error bound.

label: Eq. 11
verbatim: min_{alpha_j} || sum_{j in C} g_{i_j} f_{i_j} - (sum_{j in C} g_{i_j}) f_tilde_C ||^2 = E[ (sum_{j in C} g_{i_j})^2 ] * Var_x[ sum_{j in C} w_j(x) f_{i_j}(x) ]
paraphrase: Minimum-L2 hierarchical merge error scales with the squared cluster-gate sum and the variance of the dynamic within-cluster mixture.
preconditions: f_tilde_C = sum_{j in C} alpha_j f_{i_j}, alpha_j >=0, sum alpha_j = 1.
consequences: Larger clusters and higher within-cluster expert diversity both inflate the merge error; explains catastrophic collapse of mega-clusters.

label: Eq. 12 (Wasserstein)
verbatim: W_1(F, F_hat) = inf_{gamma in Gamma(mu,nu)} sum_{i,j} gamma_{ij} * (1/pi) * arccos( (f_i . f_hat_j) / (||f_i|| ||f_hat_j||) )
paraphrase: 1-Wasserstein distance between original and compressed expert output manifolds with normalized angular cost.
preconditions: F = original outputs, F_hat = K/2 compressed outputs; mu, nu uniform measures over indices; transport plan gamma in Gamma(mu,nu).
consequences: Used to quantify manifold distortion under merging vs pruning (Figure 2).

label: Eq. 13 (EAN saliency)
verbatim: S_i = sum_{x in X_i} ||f_i(x)||_2
paraphrase: EAN saliency: SUM (not mean) of expert activation L2-norms over tokens where the expert is active. No router-gate weighting; no conditional normalization by |X_i|.
preconditions: X_i = tokens where expert i is in TopK(g(x)).
consequences: EAN's global accumulation entangles functional impact with activation frequency; baseline that REAP outperforms.

label: Eq. 14 (M-SMoE distance)
paraphrase: M-SMoE pairwise expert distance is the mean cosine distance of router gate-value vectors over the calibration set.
preconditions: Gates collected on full X_cal; dominant experts identified by global usage frequency nu.
consequences: Cluster initialisation: non-dominant expert j assigned to dominant expert with smallest D_{i,j}.

label: Eq. 15 (frequency-weighted merge)
verbatim: W_tilde_a = ( sum_{i in C_alpha} nu_i W'_i ) / ( sum_{i in C_alpha} nu_i )
paraphrase: Merged expert weights are the frequency-weighted average of permuted constituent expert weights within cluster C_alpha.
preconditions: Permutation P_j (M-SMoE) or identity (HC-SMoE) applied first; nu_i = expert usage frequency.
consequences: Used by both M-SMoE and HC-SMoE to produce the merged expert.

label: Eq. 16 (HC-SMoE distance)
verbatim: D_{i,j} = 1 - (A_i . A_j) / (||A_i|| ||A_j||),   A_i := E_{x ~ X_cal}[f_i(x)] = (1/|X_cal|) sum_{x in X_cal} f_i(x)
paraphrase: HC-SMoE clusters experts using cosine distance between representative vectors A_i (mean activation over the entire calibration set).
preconditions: Average linkage hierarchical agglomerative clustering; singletons initialised per expert.
consequences: Spec uses HC-SMoE only as comparison baseline, not as Stage 2 method.

label: M-SMoE permutation
verbatim: W'^{(j)}_up = W^{(j)}_up P_j,  W'^{(j)}_gate = W^{(j)}_gate P_j,  W'^{(j)}_down = P_j^T W^{(j)}_down
paraphrase: Weight-matching permutation P_j applied to the intermediate dimension of expert j (SwiGLU up/gate left-multiplied; down right-permuted by P_j^T) leaves expert outputs invariant.
preconditions: Each expert is a two-layer SwiGLU block {W_up, W_gate, W_down}; P_j found by Git Re-Basin weight-matching aligning f_j to f_i.
consequences: Permutation invariance preserved before frequency-weighted averaging.

label: Frequency baseline
verbatim: The frequency-based pruning saliency criterion prunes experts with the lowest usage frequency across the calibration dataset. The saliency of f_i is simply S_i = nu_i.
paraphrase: Baseline saliency = expert usage count nu_i over X_cal.
preconditions: nu_i = |X_i|.
consequences: Naive baseline; ignores both gate value g_j and ||f_j||, fails at scale.

label: Active-gate normalization
verbatim: We assume the active gates are normalized such that sum_{k in T(x)} g_k(x) = 1, an operation commonly included in SMoE architectures.
paraphrase: Theory and REAP saliency assume top-k gates are renormalized to sum to 1 per token.
preconditions: Models studied include this normalization (Mixtral, Qwen3, ERNIE, GLM, Llama-4, Qwen3-Coder, Kimi-K2).
consequences: When implementing REAP, gate values used in S_j must be the post-softmax/post-renormalization gates, not raw router logits.

label: REAP minimizes Eq.8 bound
verbatim: directly minimizing the pruned expert's impact g_j(x) ||f_j(x)|| is an effective heuristic to minimize the total error. This strategy targets the known components of the error bound (g_j ||f_j||) while simultaneously shrinking the scaling coefficient (g_j) of the unknown component (||f_i||).
paraphrase: Since promoted expert i (and ||f_i||) is unknown across tokens, minimizing g_j||f_j|| both targets the known terms of Eq.8 and shrinks the coefficient of the unknown ||f_i|| term.
preconditions: Top-k routing with promoted gate g'_i <= g_j (Eq. 8).
consequences: Theoretical justification for the REAP saliency choice.

label: Calibration <= 110B
verbatim: For calibration, 1,024 samples are randomly selected and packed to 2,048 sequence length for models with <= 110B parameters.
paraphrase: Small/mid models: 1,024 calibration samples, packed to seq-len 2,048.
preconditions: Models with parameter count <= 110B (Qwen3-30B, Mixtral-8x7B, ERNIE-4.5-21B, GLM-4.5-Air-106B, Llama-4-Scout-107B).
consequences: Spec must use exactly these calibration sizes when running REAP on these scales.

label: Calibration >= 110B
verbatim: For models with >= 110B parameters, we select 12,228 samples with a maximum sequence length of 16,384 tokens without truncation or packing.
paraphrase: Large models: 12,228 samples, max seq-len 16,384, no truncation, no packing.
preconditions: Parameters >= 110B (note: 12,228 = oddly-spelled, paper text; double-check vs 12288). Qwen3-Coder-480B, Kimi-K2.
consequences: Spec calibration sizes must match for large-scale runs.

label: Compression ratios
verbatim: We compress models by pruning or merging 25% or 50% of experts in each layer, except for M-SMoE which determines the number of clusters per layer based on global expert usage frequency.
paraphrase: Per-layer pruning ratios: 25% or 50% (REAP, EAN, frequency, HC-SMoE). M-SMoE is non-uniform per layer.
preconditions: One-shot setting, no fine-tuning post-compression.
consequences: REAP applied UNIFORMLY per layer at 25% or 50%.

label: Seeds <= 50B
verbatim: When evaluating models with <= 50B parameters on coding and MC, we calibrate and compress the models using three different seeds and report the mean.
paraphrase: <=50B coding/MC: 3 seeds, mean reported.
preconditions: Coding (EvalPlus, LiveCodeBench) and MC suites only.
consequences: Larger models, creative writing, and math: single seed.

label: One-shot
verbatim: All models are evaluated in the one-shot setting, with no additional fine-tuning after compression.
paraphrase: No post-compression fine-tuning is performed in any reported result.
preconditions: Calibration only, no SFT/distillation.
consequences: Establishes one-shot pruning regime; spec must match for direct comparison.

label: Calibration datasets
verbatim: For MC question answering and code generation benchmarks, we use C4 ... and evol-codealpaca ... datasets to assess both general and domain-specific calibration. Models with >= 110B parameters are additionally calibrated with data from xlam-function-calling ... and SWE-smith-trajectories. For creative writing and math benchmarks we employ WritingPrompts curated and tulu-3-sft-personas-math, respectively.
paraphrase: Calibration datasets: C4, evol-codealpaca, xlam-function-calling, SWE-smith-trajectories, WritingPrompts curated, tulu-3-sft-personas-math; chat template applied; reasoning disabled (</think>).
preconditions: Domain-matched calibration; default chat template; reasoning closed via </think> tags for hybrid models.
consequences: Domain-specific calibration is critical (cf. Figure A8 — C4 calibration collapses code accuracy).

label: Disable reasoning
verbatim: </think> tags are explicitly closed to disable reasoning in hybrid reasoning models. ... For hybrid reasoning models such as Qwen3-30B-A3B, we disable reasoning on all tasks by setting enable_thinking=False in the chat template.
paraphrase: Hybrid reasoning models (e.g. Qwen3) have thinking explicitly disabled both at calibration (close </think>) and at evaluation (enable_thinking=False).
preconditions: Hybrid reasoning model architectures.
consequences: Calibration activations must be collected with reasoning OFF for fair comparison.

label: Generation config <=110B
verbatim: For models with <= 110B parameters, we use greedy sampling (i.e, temperature = 0.0) to evaluate code generation and math reasoning. For creative writing we use the default temperature, top-P, and top-K settings for each respective model. The maximum number of output tokens is extended to 16,384 for all generative tasks.
paraphrase: <=110B: greedy decoding for code+math, default temp/top-p/top-k for creative writing, max 16,384 output tokens.
preconditions: Generative benchmarks; not MC.
consequences: Reproducing REAP requires greedy decoding for code/math eval.

label: Generation config >=110B
verbatim: For larger models with >= 110B parameters, we use greedy sampling for EvalPlus, SWE-Bench, and BFCLv3. On LiveCodeBench, Qwen3-Coder-480B and Kimi-K2 are evaluated with default sampling parameters and greedy sampling, respectively. We report the mean and standard deviation for Qwen3-Coder-480B on LiveCodeBench over five random seeds. We use a repetition penalty of 1.05 for all large model evaluations. For EvalPlus we use 768 as the maximum number of output tokens and 16,384 for LiveCodeBench. For BFCLv3 we set the maximum number of output tokens to 4,096.
paraphrase: Large-model eval config: greedy for most; LiveCodeBench mixed; rep_penalty=1.05; EvalPlus max 768 tokens, LiveCodeBench 16,384, BFCLv3 4,096; 5 seeds for Qwen3-Coder-480B LiveCodeBench.
preconditions: >=110B parameter models.
consequences: Spec eval configs must match for parity.

label: SWE-Bench eval
verbatim: We use 4,096 and 16,384 as the maximum number of output tokens for evaluating Qwen3-Coder-480B and Kimi-K2-Instruct-W4A16 on SWE-Bench, respectively. The input context length for both models is limited to 65,536. ... We set the maximum number of restarts to 20.
paraphrase: SWE-Bench: max output 4,096 (Qwen3-Coder) / 16,384 (Kimi-K2); context 65,536; up to 20 restarts.
preconditions: mini-SWE-agent scaffolding; SWE-Bench Verified test set.
consequences: Reproduction requirement.

label: Table 1 - SMoE models
verbatim: ERNIE-4.5-21B-A3B-PT (64 routed, 2 shared, top-6, 87.88% sparsity, 21.9B/3B, first-layer dense=Yes, granularity=High); Qwen3-30B-A3B (128, 0, 8, 93.75%, 30.5/3, No, High); Mixtral-8x7B-Instruct-v0.1 (8, 0, 2, 75.00%, 46.7/13, No, Low); GLM-4.5-Air (128, 1, 8, 93.02%, 106.9/12, Yes, High); Llama-4-Scout-17B-16E-Instruct (16, 1, 1, 88.24%, 107.8/17, No, Low); Qwen3-Coder-480B-A35B-Instruct-FP8 (160, 0, 8, 95.00%, 480.2/35, No, High); Kimi-K2-Instruct-W4A16 (384, 1, 8, 97.66%, 1026.4/32, Yes, High).
paraphrase: Architectural specs for the seven SMoE models studied.
preconditions: RedHatAI 2025 W4A16 quantization for Kimi-K2.
consequences: Defines target architectures Stage 2 must support.

label: Table 2 - Qwen3-30B 50% REAP
verbatim: Qwen3-30B-A3B Baseline: Eval+ 0.814, LiveCode 0.302, Coding Avg 0.558, WildBench 0.811, GSM8K 0.903, MATH-500 0.872, Math Avg 0.887, MC Avg 0.721. REAP 50%: Eval+ 0.780, LiveCode 0.302, Coding Avg 0.541, WildBench 0.718, GSM8K 0.877, MATH-500 0.838, Math Avg 0.857, MC Avg 0.503.
paraphrase: REAP 50% on Qwen3-30B preserves coding nearly fully (0.541 vs baseline 0.558) but MC drops to 0.503 from 0.721.
preconditions: Domain-specific calibration; 3-seed mean.
consequences: Anchor result for Stage 2 Qwen3 acceptance.

label: Table 2 - GLM-4.5-Air 50% REAP
verbatim: GLM-4.5-Air Baseline: Eval+ 0.786, LiveCode 0.374, Coding 0.580, WildBench 0.839, GSM8K 0.846, MATH-500 0.918, Math 0.882, MC 0.747. REAP 50%: Eval+ 0.679, LiveCode 0.352, Coding 0.515, WildBench 0.754, GSM8K 0.815, MATH-500 0.900, Math 0.857, MC 0.554.
paraphrase: REAP 50% on GLM-4.5-Air maintains math near-baseline (0.857 vs 0.882), drops MC to 0.554.
preconditions: Domain-specific calibration.
consequences: Anchor result for GLM-4.5-Air.

label: Table 3 - Qwen3-Coder-480B 50% REAP
verbatim: Qwen3-Coder-480B baseline: Eval+ 0.841, LiveCode 0.431, Coding 0.636, SWE-Bench 0.540, BFCL Non-Live 0.866, Live 0.825, Multi-Turn 0.380, Overall 0.690, MC 0.750. REAP 50%: Eval+ 0.822, LiveCode 0.415, Coding 0.619, SWE-Bench 0.522, BFCL Non-Live 0.849, Live 0.801, Multi-Turn 0.371, Overall 0.674, MC 0.692.
paraphrase: Near-lossless on coding (0.619 vs 0.636 baseline) at 50% pruning of Qwen3-Coder-480B.
preconditions: FP8 quantization; domain-specific calibration.
consequences: Demonstrates REAP scales to 480B-class models.

label: Table 3 - Kimi-K2 50% REAP
verbatim: Kimi-K2-Instruct-W4A16 baseline: Eval+ 0.828, LiveCode 0.434, Coding 0.631, SWE-Bench 0.554, BFCL Overall 0.666, MC 0.780. REAP 50%: Eval+ 0.819, LiveCode 0.429, Coding 0.624, SWE-Bench 0.576, BFCL Overall 0.564, MC 0.643.
paraphrase: Kimi-K2 1T-class model: REAP 50% retains 0.624 coding (vs 0.631), even gains on SWE-Bench (0.576 vs 0.554).
preconditions: W4A16 (INT4) quantization; calibration includes SWE-smith and xlam.
consequences: REAP 50% on quantized 1T models is near-lossless for coding.

label: MC mean accuracy drop
verbatim: HC-SMoE and REAP have a mean decrease in accuracy of approximately 4% and 13% for compression ratios of 25% and 50%, respectively, excluding large-scale SMoEs.
paraphrase: Across small/mid models on MC, both HC-SMoE and REAP drop ~4% at 25% pruning and ~13% at 50% pruning.
preconditions: Excluding Qwen3-Coder-480B and Kimi-K2.
consequences: REAP and HC-SMoE comparable on MC; merging holds up on discriminative tasks.

label: Coding mean drop REAP
verbatim: Excluding large-scale SMoEs, REAP achieves a mean decrease in accuracy of 1.9% and 6.9% at 25% and 50% compression ratios, respectively, on coding. In comparison, both HC-SMoE and M-SMoE produce mean decreases in accuracy >5% at 25% compression and >20% at 50% compression.
paraphrase: REAP coding drops: 1.9% (25%) and 6.9% (50%); merging methods >5% and >20%.
preconditions: Generative coding benchmarks (EvalPlus + LiveCodeBench), small/mid models.
consequences: Pruning is decisively superior to merging on generative coding.

label: Math at 25%
verbatim: REAP attains the best mathematical reasoning results with a remarkable mean decrease in accuracy of just 0.1% at 25% compression.
paraphrase: REAP math eval at 25% pruning: 0.1% mean accuracy drop.
preconditions: GSM8K + MATH-500 with evalscope.
consequences: Near-lossless math at 25%.

label: Near-lossless large-scale coding
verbatim: On non-agentic coding tasks, REAP achieves near-lossless accuracy with a 0.16% and 1.2% mean decrease in accuracy compared to baseline at 25% and 50%, respectively, outperforming EAN and frequency-based pruning, particularly at 50% compression.
paraphrase: Qwen3-Coder-480B + Kimi-K2 non-agentic coding: REAP drops 0.16% at 25% and 1.2% at 50%.
preconditions: Large-scale pruning of Qwen3-Coder and Kimi-K2.
consequences: REAP is the production-grade scalable pruning criterion.

label: Tool-use BFCLv3
verbatim: On tool use, EAN and REAP are comparable, with REAP slightly outperforming at 50% compression with a mean decrease in accuracy of 5.9% versus 6.2% for EAN.
paraphrase: BFCLv3 50%: REAP 5.9% mean drop, EAN 6.2%.
preconditions: Tool-calling evaluation.
consequences: REAP marginally beats EAN on tool calling.

label: Frequency pruning collapses
verbatim: Frequency-based pruning suffers from a sharp degradation in quality at 50% compression, highlighting the importance of pruning saliency criteria which consider expert activations.
paraphrase: Frequency-only saliency catastrophically fails at 50% pruning of large models.
preconditions: Qwen3-Coder-480B and Kimi-K2 50% pruning.
consequences: Saliency criteria must consider expert activation magnitudes.

label: Pruning + quantization
verbatim: pruning can be easily applied to quantized models without any additional steps required to reconcile block scales or re-quantize following compression. ... combining expert merging with quantization is more complex; the fine-grained shared quantization scales across parameter groups must be reconciled during merging, whereas pruning requires no such adjustment.
paraphrase: Pruning composes trivially with block-quantized models; merging requires re-quantization of merged weights.
preconditions: Block quantization formats (e.g. W4A16-G128, FP8).
consequences: REAP works on FP8 / W4A16 checkpoints out-of-the-box; merging does not.

label: Table A5 - Q3 quant+REAP
verbatim: Qwen3-30B-A3B EvalPlus: W16A16 128 experts 100% size 81.4; W16A16 64 experts 50% 78.0; W4A16-G128 128 experts 25% 80.5; W4A16-G128 64 experts 12.5% 77.6; W2A16-G128 64 experts 12.5% 28.6.
paraphrase: REAP 50% expert pruning combined with W4A16 yields 12.5% relative size at EvalPlus 77.6 — vs W2A16 alone 28.6.
preconditions: LLM Compressor; evol-codealpaca-v1 calibration.
consequences: Pruning + 4-bit quantization beats 2-bit quantization alone.

label: C4 calibration collapses code
verbatim: C4 calibration results in a collapse in accuracy, with several compressed model instances failing to produce coherent outputs, resulting in 0% accuracy.
paraphrase: Out-of-domain (C4) calibration produces 0% coding accuracy for some compressed instances.
preconditions: Code generation evaluation.
consequences: Stage 2 must use domain-matched calibration; never C4 alone for code.

label: Late-layer collapse
verbatim: Early layers ... exhibit lower policy variability and thus less dramatic collapse. Late layers, where experts have specialized for distinct computational roles, demonstrate high policy variability, resulting in the severe functional collapse observed when these specialized experts are merged into static averages.
paraphrase: Functional subspace collapse is concentrated in late layers (high Var[r]); early layers remain stable.
preconditions: High-granularity SMoEs (Qwen3, ERNIE-4.5).
consequences: Validates Eq. 6 prediction: error proportional to Var[r(x)].

label: Table A4 - PCA variance
paraphrase: PC1+PC2 cumulative variance: merged consistently exceeds baseline (loss of high-dim complexity); pruned consistently below baseline (preserves outliers).
preconditions: PCA on mean expert activations from 32 samples * 2048 tokens of C4.
consequences: Quantitative evidence of subspace collapse under merging.

label: Manifold analysis setup
verbatim: We analyse the functional expert output manifolds across four diverse state-of-the-art SMoE architectures by recording mean expert activations from 32 samples of 2048 tokens from the C4 dataset.
paraphrase: Manifold analysis: 32 samples * 2048 tokens from C4; mean expert activations.
preconditions: Qwen3-30B, ERNIE-4.5-21B, Mixtral-8x7B, Llama-4-Scout.
consequences: Defines the empirical setup behind Figures 1, 2, A5, and Table A4.

label: Singleton clusters
verbatim: When merging works well, it's more closely related to pruning than one might expect ... HC-SMoE in particular has a high prevalence of singleton clusters, leaving important experts unadulterated and compressing the rest into a few mega-clusters containing tens of experts.
paraphrase: HC-SMoE produces many singleton clusters (effectively pruning) plus mega-clusters; merging only succeeds where it imitates pruning.
preconditions: High-granularity models.
consequences: Reinforces that pruning is the fundamental winning operation.

label: Summed-gate merging flawed
verbatim: we conclude that merging with summed gates is fundamentally flawed whenever: (i) the router has learned an input-dependent policy for mixing two experts (Var[r] > 0); and (ii) the experts are themselves distinct (||Delta_ij|| > 0). Any fixed alpha cannot overcome the irreducible error bound established in Equation (6).
paraphrase: Summed-gate merging is irreducibly lossy under (i) Var[r]>0 and (ii) ||Delta_ij||>0.
preconditions: Both conditions typically hold for high-granularity MoE.
consequences: Theoretical foundation for preferring pruning.

label: Pruning preserves independent control
verbatim: Pruning removes one function but importantly does not tie the remaining gates. The router still modulates each surviving expert independently. In contrast, merging removes a degree of freedom in the policy by replacing individual experts with their mergers.
paraphrase: Pruning is a coordinate-subspace operation: surviving experts retain independent gating; merging ties gates and reduces policy DoF.
preconditions: Top-k routing.
consequences: Architectural argument for pruning over merging.

label: EvalPlus output tokens (large)
verbatim: For EvalPlus we use 768 as the maximum number of output tokens and 16,384 for LiveCodeBench.
paraphrase: Large-model EvalPlus max output = 768 tokens; LiveCodeBench = 16,384.
preconditions: >=110B models.
consequences: Reproduction config.

label: Repetition penalty 1.05
verbatim: We use a repetition penalty of 1.05 for all large model evaluations.
paraphrase: All >=110B model evals use rep_penalty=1.05.
preconditions: >=110B models.
consequences: Spec eval config.

label: Creative writing 25%
verbatim: On creative writing, REAP and EAN are near-lossless at 25% compression with REAP offering improved quality at 50% compression.
paraphrase: WildBench creative writing: REAP & EAN near-lossless at 25%; REAP wins at 50%.
preconditions: 146 WildBench prompts judged by gpt-4o-2024-05-13.
consequences: Creative writing tracks coding pattern.

label: HC-SMoE linkage
verbatim: We implement HC-SMoE and M-SMoE as expert merging baselines. Average linkage criterion is used for HC-SMoE. M-SMoE does not include low-rank compression from the complete MC-SMoE method.
paraphrase: HC-SMoE: average linkage agglomerative clustering. M-SMoE: parameter-only (no low-rank stage).
preconditions: Baseline merging implementations.
consequences: Defines exact baseline configuration for comparison.

label: JSD divergence over tokens
verbatim: as the completion token position increases the merged models diverge from the baseline more rapidly than the REAP pruned model.
paraphrase: Per-token Jensen-Shannon divergence: merged models diverge from baseline far faster than REAP-pruned over completion length.
preconditions: 100 evol-codealpaca prompts on 50% compressed Qwen3-30B; comparison vs baseline logits.
consequences: REAP outputs remain aligned with baseline auto-regressively.
